title: Fears And Scruples
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hereן¿½s my case. Of old I used to love him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This same unseen friend, before I knew:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dream there was none like him, none above him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wake to hope and trust my dream was true.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loved I not his letters full of beauty?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not his actions famous far and wide?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Absent, he would know I vowed him duty;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Present, he would find me at his side.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pleasant fancy! for I had but letters,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only knew of actions by hearsay:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He himself was busied with my betters;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What of that? My turn must come some day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Some dayן¿½ proving, no day! Hereן¿½s the puzzle.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Passed and passed my turn is. Why complain?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s so busied! If I could but muzzle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peopleן¿½s foolish mouths that give me pain!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Letters?ן¿½ (hear them!) ן¿½You a judge of writing?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ask the experts! How they shake the head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er these characters, your friendן¿½s inditing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Call them forgery from A to Z!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Actions? Whereן¿½s your certain proofן¿½ (they bother)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He, of all you find so great and good,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He, he only, claims this, that, the other
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Action, claimed by men, a multitude?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I can simply wish I might refute you,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wish my friend would, by a word, a wink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bid me stop that foolish mouth, you brute you!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He keeps absent, why, I cannot think.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never mind! Though foolishness may flout me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One thingן¿½s sure enough: ן¿½tis neither frost,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No, nor fire, shall freeze or burn from out me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thanks for truth, though falsehood, gained, though lost.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All my days, Iן¿½ll go the softlier, sadlier,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For that dreamן¿½s sake! How forget the thrill
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through and through me as I thought ן¿½The gladlier
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lives my friend because I love him still!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, but thereן¿½s a menace some one utters!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What and if your friend at home play tricks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peep at hide-and-seek behind the shutters?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mean your eyes should pierce through solid bricks?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What and if he, frowning, wake you, dreamy?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lay on you the blame that bricks, conceal?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Say ן¿½At least I saw who did not see me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Does see now, and presently shall feelן¿½?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why, that makes your friend a monster!ן¿½ say you:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Had his house no window? At first nod,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would you not have hailed him?ן¿½ Hush, I pray you!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What if this friend happened to be God?
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
